candidate_problems.text: Can the conjecture be made precise enough to distinguish 'formal' from 'dynamical' orthogonality in a testable way? The central open problem is to define coherence, stability, competitiveness, and goal invariance under self-modification and ontological change, so that the claim becomes more than an intuitive selection story. Without such a formalization, it is unclear whether the conjecture identifies a real structural constraint or merely redescribes familiar robustness problems.

Is computational irreducibility actually the causal source of narrowing goal-capability pairings, or would similar convergence arise from more general features of agency such as bounded optimization, embeddedness, resource limits, and evolutionary/game-theoretic selection? The unresolved tension is whether irreducibility is doing distinctive explanatory work here or whether it is an unnecessary overlay on already-known pressures toward corrigibility, world-model updating, and instrumental convergence.

What kinds of terminal goals are genuinely 'computationally robust' across ontology shifts, edge cases, and recursive self-improvement, and do any nontrivial examples exist? The conjecture predicts a narrowed viable class, but it remains open whether that class is broad, sparse, or collapses into mostly meta-goals like preserving optionality, maintaining search, or protecting epistemic integrity. This matters because the conjecture is only substantively interesting if it yields a nontrivial taxonomy of survivable goals rather than a vague claim that brittle goals fail.
